design_note: Stage 3 grammar spiral. Each structure has a primary teaching unit, a list of reinforce units (where it reappears in passages/games), and an anticipation unit (where it appears passively before explicit teaching). All 26 A1 Movers grammar structures from the official Cambridge list are placed.
reference: Cambridge A1 Movers Grammar and Structures List (free public PDF, 2 pages)

structures.label: Verb 'be' — affirmative, negative, questions, short answers
structures.notes: Foundation — already in Starters; Stage 3 Unit 1 brings full conjugation incl. plural + negative contractions

structures.label: Be called + noun
structures.example: I am called Linh.

structures.label: Possessive 's + possessive pronouns (mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs)
structures.example: That is my brother's bag. — Yes, it's his.

structures.label: have got / has got — affirmative, negative, questions
structures.example: I've got a new bag. — Has she got a sister?

structures.label: have to / had to (obligation)
structures.example: I have to do my homework. — She had to wear a uniform yesterday.

structures.label: there is / there are (affirmative, negative, questions)
structures.example: There is a balcony in our flat. — Are there any books?

structures.label: can / can't / could (ability + permission)
structures.example: I can swim. — Could you ride a bike when you were five?

structures.label: must / mustn't (obligation, prohibition)
structures.example: You must finish your work. — You mustn't run in the hall.

structures.label: Shall I / Shall we ...? (offers + suggestions)
structures.example: Shall I help you, Mum? — Shall we go to the park?

structures.label: Indirect object (give him a book / give a book to him)
structures.example: Pass me the salt. — Give the present to your friend.

structures.label: Countable / uncountable nouns + some / any / a lot of / a few / a bit of
structures.example: There are some sandwiches. — Is there any juice?

structures.label: Prepositions of time (at + clock, on + days, in + month, after, before)
structures.example: Class starts at nine. — On Saturdays I play football.

structures.label: Prepositions of place (above, below, between, behind, in front of, near, opposite, next to)
structures.example: The shop is opposite the bank. — There's a balcony above the door.

structures.label: Present simple — affirmative, negative, questions, short answers
structures.example: I get up at seven. — She doesn't drink coffee. — Do they play tennis?

structures.label: Adverbs of frequency (always, usually, often, sometimes, never)
structures.example: I always have breakfast. — They sometimes go to the cinema.

structures.label: Question words (what, when, where, who, why, which, how, how much, how many, how often, how old)
structures.example: Why do you like art? — How often do you swim?

structures.label: like / love / hate + -ing
structures.example: I love swimming. — She hates doing homework.

structures.label: Present continuous — affirmative, negative, questions
structures.example: I am reading a comic. — Are they playing in the garden?

structures.label: Present simple vs present continuous
structures.example: I usually walk to school but today I'm taking the bus.

structures.label: Imperatives (affirmative, negative)
structures.example: Wait for the bus. — Don't cross now.

structures.label: Conjunctions: and, but, or, because
structures.example: I'm hungry because I didn't eat lunch.

structures.label: When clauses (not future meaning)
structures.example: When I got home, I had dinner.

structures.label: Comparative adjectives (short + long, irregular) + 'than'
structures.example: My brother is taller than me. — This book is more interesting than that one.

structures.label: Superlative adjectives (-est, most-, irregular)
structures.example: Mount Fansipan is the tallest mountain in Vietnam.

structures.label: Comparative + superlative adverbs (more quickly, best)
structures.example: She speaks more quickly than her brother. — He sings best in our class.

structures.label: Verb + infinitive (want to, would like to, start to, try to)
structures.example: I want to buy a new ball. — She'd like to go home.

structures.label: Want/ask someone to do something
structures.example: Mum wants me to tidy my room. — Ask your teacher to help you.

structures.label: Infinitive of purpose (to + verb)
structures.example: I went to the library to read.

structures.label: Verb + ing (after some verbs — went riding, kept walking)
structures.example: We went swimming on Sunday.

structures.label: going to — future plans
structures.example: It's going to rain. — I'm going to visit my grandmother.

structures.label: Past simple — was / were (affirmative, negative, questions)
structures.example: It was a great party. — Were you at school yesterday?

structures.label: Past simple — regular (-ed) + common irregular (went, saw, had, did, made, gave, ate, came)
structures.bridge_to: Stage 4 Flyers — past simple full + present perfect intro

structures.label: Relative clauses (who, which, where)
structures.example: This is the boy who lives next door. — That's the park where we play.

structures.label: What is / was the weather like?
structures.example: What's the weather like today? — What was it like at the beach?

structures.label: What's the matter?
structures.example: What's the matter? — I've got a headache.

structures.label: How about / What about + n or ing
structures.example: How about going to the cinema? — What about pizza for dinner?

structures.label: Go for a + n (go for a walk / drive / swim)
structures.example: Let's go for a walk in the park.

structures.label: Be good at + n / ing
structures.example: She is good at swimming. — I'm good at maths.

structures.label: I think / I know + clause
structures.example: I think he is nice. — I don't know where she is.

summary.movers_official_count_aligned: All 26 structures from Cambridge official A1 Movers Grammar List are mapped; 13 supplementary placements (review/reinforce) added for spiral effect.
